    Orbital Anisotropy in Galaxy Clusters

    Orbital Anisotropy

    Type:Dynamical property
    Parameter:β(r) = 1 − σθ²/σr²
    β = 0:Isotropic orbits
    β → 1:Purely radial orbits
    β < 0:Tangential orbits
    Related: Jeans Equation
    MAMPOSSt
    Ram-Pressure Stripping

    Orbital anisotropy β(r) quantifies whether galaxy orbits in a cluster are predominantly radial, tangential, or isotropic [1]. It is a key diagnostic of cluster assembly history and environmental processing: infalling galaxies tend to have radial orbits, while virialised populations are more isotropic [1][2].

    Radial Trends

    Both observations and simulations consistently show anisotropy growing with radius — from near-isotropy at the cluster centre to predominantly radial orbits in the outskirts [3][4][5][6]. This reflects the transition from a virialised core to an infall-dominated periphery [2].

    Population Dependence

    The key finding of Pedratti et al. (2026) is that anisotropy profiles differ by galaxy colour [7]:

    • Blue cloud galaxies: More radial orbits at all radii, consistent with recent infall [7][2]
    • Red sequence galaxies: More isotropic, consistent with virialisation [7]
    • Green valley galaxies: Intermediate behaviour [7]

    This pattern is consistent with ram-pressure stripping along radial orbits quenching blue infalling galaxies into the green valley and eventually the red sequence [7][8][9]. Mamon et al. (2019) found similar trends in stacked WINGS clusters [2], and Biviano et al. (2025) confirmed this in CLASH-VLT clusters [10]. However, Hwang & Lee (2008) reported no significant differences [11], suggesting this may depend on sample size or cluster dynamical state.

    Parametric Models

    • Osipkov-Merritt: β(r) = r²/(r² + rβ²), widely used for its simplicity [12][13]
    • Tiret (generalised): Captures transitions between central isotropy and outer radial orbits with more flexibility [14]

    See Also

    • Jeans Equation
    • Coma Cluster
    • Projected Phase Space

    References
    1. Mamon, G. A., Biviano, A., Boué, G. (2013). "MAMPOSSt." MNRAS, 429, 3079.
    2. Mamon, G. A., et al. (2019). "Orbital anisotropy in WINGS clusters." A&A. DOI: 10.1051/0004-6361/201935081
    3. Host, O., et al. (2009). "Observational anisotropy profiles in clusters." ApJ.
    4. Biviano, A., et al. (2013). "Cluster anisotropy profiles." A&A.
    5. Hansen, S. H., et al. (2006). "Anisotropy in simulated clusters." ApJ.
    6. Mamon, G. A., et al. (2010). "Simulated cluster anisotropy." A&A.
    7. Pedratti, S., et al. (2026). "Coma Cluster kinematics with DESI. I." arXiv:2603.16706
    8. Gunn, J. E., Gott, J. R. (1972). "On the Infall of Matter into Clusters." ApJ, 176, 1.
    9. Vollmer, B., et al. (2001). "Ram-pressure stripping on radial orbits." ApJ.
    10. Biviano, A., et al. (2025). "CLASH-VLT cluster anisotropy." arXiv:2508.05195
    11. Hwang, H. S., Lee, M. G. (2008). "Galaxy orbits in clusters." ApJ.
    12. Osipkov, L. P. (1979). "Spherical systems with anisotropic velocity." Pisma Astron. Zh., 5, 77.
    13. Merritt, D. (1985). "Spherical stellar systems." AJ, 90, 1027.
    14. Tiret, O., et al. (2007). "Generalised Tiret anisotropy model." A&A.
    Tags: Galaxy Clusters Galaxy Evolution
